title: How the sensation of being restrained activates primal terror
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The body remembers what the mind tries to forget 

 I was lying on my back in a yoga class. The instructor came over and gently pressed my shoulders down...
htmlContent:
The body remembers what the mind tries to forget

I was lying on my back in a yoga class. The instructor came over and gently pressed my shoulders down toward the mat. Just a small adjustment. Something most people would barely notice. But my entire nervous system went offline. My breath stopped. My vision tunneled. I felt like I was being buried alive.

I got up and walked out. Sat in my car shaking for twenty minutes. Couldn't figure out why a friendly touch on my shoulders made me want to crawl out of my skin.

Here's the thing. That sensation of being held down, even gently, even with good intentions, activates something ancient and wordless in the body. It's not about the person touching you. It's about the nervous system's memory of restraint. And for those of us with complex trauma, that memory is not stored as a story. It's stored as a reflex.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say my dorsal vagal nerve had just slammed on the brakes. That's the oldest part of our nervous system. The part that goes back to our reptilian ancestors. When it detects immobilization, even the hint of it, it reads that as life-threatening. Because in the wild, being pinned down means being eaten. So the body shuts everything down. Freezes. Plays dead. Right?

And here's the part that gets me. Most people have no idea this is happening inside them. They just know they hate crowded rooms. Or they can't stand having their wrists touched. Or they panic when someone hugs them from behind. The mind makes up reasons. "I'm just not a hug person." "I like my personal space." But the body knows the truth. It's remembering something the conscious mind never fully processed.

The science of being held down

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades studying how animals and humans process threat. He noticed something crucial. In the wild, a prey animal that gets caught will freeze completely. Its body goes rigid. Its heart rate drops. It feels nothing. This is nature's anesthetic. But once the predator leaves, the animal shakes. Trembles. Completes the escape response. And then goes back to grazing like nothing happened.

Humans don't do that. We freeze. And then we stay frozen. Because the threat doesn't leave. Or it left a long time ago, but the nervous system never got the memo. So the sensation of being restrained, even lightly, can trigger that incomplete freeze response. The body goes right back to that moment when escape wasn't possible.

Think about it. A partner playfully pins your wrists during sex. A dentist holds your jaw open. A massage therapist puts too much pressure on your back. A doctor holds you down for a procedure. In each of these situations, the conscious mind knows this is safe. But the nervous system doesn't speak English. It speaks in pressure, temperature, and movement. And when it feels restraint, it asks one question: "Can I get away?"

If the answer is no, even for a second, the alarm bells ring. Cortisol floods the system. The heart races. Or it slows to a dangerous crawl. The mind dissociates. You're still there, but you're not really there. And afterward, you feel exhausted, ashamed, confused about why something so minor affected you so deeply.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower ambient sound without completely blocking it. That can help when sensory overwhelm makes you feel trapped in your own body. Because sometimes the feeling of being restrained isn't physical at all. It's auditory. It's the sound of a room that won't stop pressing in on you.

Why your childhood bedroom might still be in your body

Janina Fisher, a leading expert on structural dissociation, writes about how trauma fragments the self into parts. One part might be the adult you, reading this article right now. Another part might be a three-year-old who was held down for a diaper change by someone who was rough. Or a six-year-old who was pinned down for a spanking. Or a teenager who was restrained during a medical procedure without anyone explaining what was happening.

That younger part is still in there. And when you feel pressure on your chest or someone grabbing your arm, that part wakes up. It doesn't know you're an adult now. It doesn't know you have choices. It just knows it's being held down again. And it reacts with the only tools it had back then. Freeze. Submit. Disappear.

This is why the sensation of being restrained can feel so primal. Because it is primal. It's not a thought. It's a survival response that bypasses the thinking brain entirely. The prefrontal cortex, the part that can reason and self-soothe, goes offline. The brainstem takes over. And the brainstem has no vocabulary. It only has action patterns.

I've worked with people who cannot tolerate wearing turtlenecks. Or seatbelts that are too tight. Or jewelry that presses against their throat. Each of these is a form of restraint. A signal to the nervous system that something is around the neck. And for someone whose trauma involved being choked, or held by the throat, or forced to swallow something, that sensation is a direct line back to the original event.

Stay with me here. This is not about being weak or overly sensitive. This is about the nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's protecting you. The problem is that it's protecting you from a threat that no longer exists. The danger is over. But the body doesn't know that. And it won't know that until you teach it, slowly and gently, that restraint does not always mean death.

What actually helps when the body says no

Recovery from this kind of trigger is not about talking yourself out of it. You can't reason with a freeze response. It doesn't respond to logic. It responds to sensation, safety, and slow movement. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote extensively about how trauma lives in the body, emphasizes that healing must be embodied. You have to work with the physical sensations, not just the story.

Here are some things that have helped me and the people I work with.

First, orienting. When you feel that panicked freeze coming on, look around the room slowly. Name five things you can see. Let your eyes move. This tells the nervous system that you are not trapped. You can see the exits. You are in the present, not in the past. It sounds too simple to work. But it does. Because the freeze response narrows your vision. Orienting widens it again.

Second, grounding through pressure. Sometimes the sensation of being restrained can be countered by applying your own pressure. Press your feet into the floor. Push your palms together. Wrap a blanket tightly around your shoulders. Give your body the sensation of containment that you control. That's the key. You are the one choosing the pressure. No one is doing it to you.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps explain why certain physical sensations feel so threatening. Because when your emotional needs were never met, your body learned that other people are not safe. And that includes their touch.

Third, pendulation. This is a term from Somatic Experiencing. It means moving between the sensation of discomfort and a sensation of safety. Maybe you feel tightness in your chest when you think about being held down. Stay with that for a few seconds. Then move your attention to something that feels good. The warmth of your hand on your belly. The feeling of your breath moving through your nostrils. Go back and forth. This teaches the nervous system that it can handle the discomfort. That it doesn't have to stay frozen forever.

Fourth, movement. The freeze response is an incomplete escape attempt. The body needs to complete it. That might mean slowly pushing your arms out as if you're pushing someone away. Or turning your head from side to side as if you're looking for an exit. Or shaking your hands and feet to release the trapped energy. Peter Levine calls this "discharging the freeze." It looks weird. It feels weird. But it works.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It goes deep into the neuroscience of how trauma gets stored physically and what helps release it. Van der Kolk's work is dense but worth the effort.

The quiet violence of well-meaning hands

I want to say something uncomfortable here. The people who restrain us are often not trying to hurt us. They are parents, doctors, partners, teachers, yoga instructors. They think they are helping. They think they are keeping us safe. And sometimes they are. But the nervous system doesn't care about intentions. It cares about pressure. It cares about . It cares about whether it can move.

I've had well-meaning friends grab my arm to keep me from walking into traffic. I'm grateful for that. But my body still reacted. My heart still pounded. I still felt that old familiar terror rising up. And then I felt ashamed for feeling terrified when someone was trying to save my life.

That shame is a second wound. It tells us that our body's reaction is wrong. That we should be grateful. That we should just relax. But the body is never wrong. It's just responding to a pattern it recognizes. The shame comes from the part of us that learned early on that our feelings were too much. That we were too much.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It offers concrete exercises for working with triggers like this one. Because understanding the theory is one thing. Having something to actually do when the freeze hits is another.

Here's what I want you to take away from this. If you have a strong reaction to being restrained, or even to the idea of it, you are not broken. You are not overreacting. You are not making it up. Your body is holding a memory that your mind has not yet been able to process. And that memory is real. It lives in your muscles, your fascia, your nervous system. It is not a character flaw. It is a survival strategy that outlived its usefulness.

The work is not to get rid of the reaction. The work is to be with it. To notice it without judging it. To let it move through you without trying to control it. To slowly, over time, teach your body that restraint can be safe. That you can be held and still be free. That you are not a prey animal anymore.

That last part is the hardest. Because somewhere inside, a part of you still believes you are. And that part needs your compassion, not your criticism. It needs you to say, "I see you. I know you're scared. I'm not going to let anyone hurt you again."

And then you have to mean it.
